data.title: Western Digital anticipates Q4 revenue $3.65B ± $100M with EPS $3.25 ± $0.15 as gross margin is guided to 51%–52%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Western Digital (WDC) Q3 fiscal 2026

Management view

  • “WD started calendar year 2026 with great execution, driving strong sequential and year-over-year revenue growth in our cloud, consumer and client businesses while expanding gross and operating margins,” said CEO Tiang Yew Tan, adding that “gross margin exceeded 50%” and that WD is “a focused HDD company and a strategic partner to hyperscalers and cloud service providers in this AI-driven data economy.”

  • Tan tied demand to AI data creation and reiterated a long-range demand view: “we expect agentic AI to drive a step function increase in capacity-oriented storage demand,” and “this reinforces our conviction that long-term data storage growth will be greater than 25% CAGR.”

  • On the product roadmap and customer qualification status, Tan said, “Our high-capacity drive road map now extends from our 44-terabyte HAMR and 40-terabyte ePMR drives that are currently in qualification to a road map that goes beyond 100-terabytes,” and added, “we are now in qualification with 4 customers” for HAMR and “qualifying our 40-terabyte ePMR drives with 3 customers and are on track to start volume production in the second half of calendar year 2026.”

  • CFO Kris Sennesael framed the quarter as execution plus customer alignment: “The WD team delivered strong results,” and reported “revenue was $3.3 billion,” “earnings per share was $2.72,” and “we delivered 222 exabytes,” while noting cloud concentration: “Cloud represented 89% of total revenue at $3 billion.”

Outlook

  • “We anticipate revenue to be $3.65 billion, plus/minus $100 million,” Sennesael said, with “gross margin ... 51% to 52%,” “operating expenses ... $385 million to $395 million,” “interest and other expenses ... $10 million,” “tax rate ... 16%,” and “diluted earnings per share ... $3.25 plus/minus $0.15.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s guidance cadence and language, management emphasized increased multi-year visibility; Tan said, “Our long-term visibility continues to improve with the duration of our agreements now extending into calendar year '28 and calendar year '29.”

Financial results

  • Sennesael reported segment revenue details: “Consumer represented 6% of revenue at $186 million,” and “Client represented 5% of total revenue at $179 million,” alongside consolidated profitability metrics including “gross margin ... 50.5%,” “operating income ... $1.3 billion,” and “operating margin of 38.6%.”

  • On cash generation and capital returns, Sennesael said, “Operating cash flow ... was $1.1 billion,” “free cash flow generation of $978 million,” and “we made $43 million of dividend payments and increased our share repurchases to $752 million,” adding, “Since the launch of our capital return program ... we have returned $2.2 billion to our shareholders.”

  • On balance sheet actions, Sennesael said WD “monetiz[ed] 5.8 million shares of SanDisk, which led to a $3.1 billion reduction in our debt,” leaving “only $1.6 billion of convertible debt,” and concluding, “we ended the quarter in a net positive cash position of $450 million.”

  • The Board action highlighted a dividend step-up: “the Board has approved a 20% increase of the cash dividend ... to $0.15 per share ... payable on June 17, 2026,” Sennesael said.

Q&A

  • Erik Woodring, Morgan Stanley: asked how “agentic AI” maps to HDD workflows and the “greater than 25%” exabyte view; CEO Tan: “We really see 3 core drivers ... training ... agentic AI and inferencing ... physical AI ... That’s why we have the confidence to see exabyte growth growing beyond 25% CAGR.”

  • Woodring, Morgan Stanley: questioned Q4 gross margin guide implying slower expansion; CFO Sennesael: “For Q4, we are guiding to 51% to 52%,” and pointed to “pricing ... mix ... and ... driv[ing] adoption of UltraSMR” as margin levers.

  • Amit Daryanani, Evercore ISI: asked what enabled the pricing step-up and whether it reflects LTAs; CEO Tan: “pricing was up 9% year-on-year,” driven by “timing of new LTAs coming on board” and “better TCO value.”

  • Daryanani, Evercore ISI: asked about cost per exabyte declines; CEO Tan: “we delivered 10% year-on-year,” and added an UltraSMR target: “by the end of fiscal year '27, close to about 60% of all the exabytes that we ship will be on UltraSMR.”

  • Aaron Rakers, Wells Fargo: asked about capacity investment needs to meet structural demand; CEO Tan: “we still do not see any need to increase unit capacity ... Our focus is really to continue to improve aerial density.”

  • Rakers, Wells Fargo: asked about capital return versus building cash; CFO Sennesael: “We are returning all the excess free cash flow back to the shareholders,” via “a combination of our dividend program and share buyback program.”

  • Thomas O’Malley, Barclays: pressed on customer appetite for continued pricing increases and LTA structures; CEO Tan: “our pricing philosophy is really to provide predictable pricing,” and said LTAs “extend into calendar year '29,” with “a degree of pricing associated with it.”

  • Michael Cadiz, Citigroup: asked on yields/reliability; CEO Tan: ePMR yields “continue to be in the 90% range,” and “we don’t see any changes.”

  • Cadiz, Citigroup: asked whether HDD demand is tied to flash/HDD price differentials and if architectures are changing; CEO Tan: “we don’t see any ... major structural changes to architecture,” and described “a very symbiotic relationship” between HDD and flash in inference stacks.

  • Samik Chatterjee, JPMorgan: asked why gross margin outperformed revenue outperformance; CFO Sennesael: “pricing ... was a little bit better ... mix ... and ... driv[ing] down costs across the board.”

  • Chatterjee, JPMorgan: asked why cost declines might not accelerate with 40TB ePMR and HAMR; CFO Sennesael: “There is going to be an adoption curve ... the improvements will be phased in over the ramp period.”

  • Aisling Grueninger, BofA: asked where UltraSMR JBOD expands reach and materiality; CEO Tan: “expand our reach into Tier 2 CSPs,” and reiterated that “close to 60% of the exabytes that we ship will be on UltraSMR” by end of fiscal 2027.

  • Christopher Muse, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked what is embedded in multi-year agreement pricing; CEO Tan: “There may be periods of pricing adjustment as we introduce new capacity points.”

  • Muse, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked about the remaining SanDisk stake timing/taxes; CFO Sennesael: “It’s our intention to monetize the remaining 1.7 million shares ... before the end of calendar year '26 ... in a tax-free manner.”

  • Karl Ackerman, BNP Paribas: asked about head/media capacity adds and prepayments; CEO Tan: “we’re ... not making any investments in terms of adding unit capacity,” but “if it makes economic sense, we’ll look to add head and media capacity.”

  • Ackerman, BNP Paribas: asked how far out commitments are POs vs LTAs; CFO Sennesael: “most of the purchase orders are being placed a year in advance,” and “beyond the first year, we are going into those ... frameworks.”

  • Hadi Orabi, TD Cowen: asked whether hyperscaler demand patterns diverge; CEO Tan: “in general, the profile is quite similar.”

  • James Schneider, Goldman Sachs: asked expected HAMR share of exabytes by end of 2027; CEO Tan: “We don’t have a number ... right now,” emphasizing a focus to “derisk the transition.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly positive, with repeated emphasis on durability and limits (pricing endurance, gross-margin sustainability, capacity investment needs), including questions such as how much “appetite do customers have to keep on taking pricing increases?” (Thomas O’Malley, Barclays) and whether there are “emerging headwinds” in margins (Erik Woodring, Morgan Stanley).

  • Management sentiment was positive in prepared remarks and remained steady in Q&A, frequently using confidence language tied to execution and visibility, including “we remain confident in our ability to deliver sustainable revenue growth” (CFO Kris Sennesael) and “we have the confidence to see exabyte growth growing beyond 25% CAGR” (CEO Tiang Yew Tan).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management used more explicit “visibility” and longer-duration agreement language (now citing extensions “into calendar year '28 and calendar year '29”), while analyst focus stayed concentrated on pricing power, margins, and the pace of technology transitions.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Strategic narrative broadened from Q2’s emphasis on being “pretty much sold out for calendar year '26” and initial qualification starts, to Q3 detailing deeper qualification scale (“in qualification with 4 customers” for HAMR) and a longer roadmap “beyond 100-terabytes.”

  • Capital allocation messaging shifted from Q2’s debt-reduction intent using SanDisk monetization to Q3’s reported completion of a large debt reduction and an explicit dividend increase; Sennesael said the Q3 SanDisk monetization “led to a $3.1 billion reduction in our debt,” and the Board approved a dividend increase to “$0.15 per share.”

  • Analyst questioning in both quarters repeatedly returned to gross-margin durability and cost-down pace, while Q3 added more direct probing of pricing step-ups and the architecture relationship between HDD and flash.

Risks and concerns

  • Management repeatedly framed execution risk around qualification and ramp timing, including yield/reliability gating for HAMR; Sennesael said there is “still a little bit of work to be done in terms of yield and reliability and quality” for HAMR even as “good progress” continues.

  • Demand fulfillment risk was addressed primarily through mix and aerial density rather than unit capacity adds; Tan said, “we still do not see any need to increase unit capacity,” and positioned “improv[ing] aerial density” and mix-up to higher capacity drives as the main mitigations.

Final takeaway

Western Digital management described Q3 as a period of strong demand and pricing with expanding profitability, and paired that with a Q4 outlook calling for higher revenue, continued gross margin expansion, and higher EPS. Executives emphasized AI-driven data creation—especially inference and agentic AI—as a durable driver of HDD capacity demand, highlighted expanding UltraSMR adoption targets, and described multi-year customer agreements extending into 2028–2029. Management also pointed to a strengthened balance sheet after SanDisk monetization, an increased dividend, and an ongoing framework to return “all the excess free cash flow” through dividends and buybacks.

data.title: UFP Industries targets $100M Deckorators growth in 2026 while doubling wood/plastic composite capacity by 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: UFP Industries (UFPI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO William Schwartz said Q1 conditions worsened as the quarter progressed: “Macro headwinds and competitive pressures increased volatility as the quarter progressed,” adding that a “longer-than-normal winter season” prevented the typical March seasonal uplift.

  • Schwartz tied profitability pressure to medical costs and framed a more guarded stance for the rest of 2026: “Business conditions have since leveled out, but given the ongoing geopolitical uncertainty and broadening inflation, particularly around higher transportation costs, we are approaching the remainder of the year with a slightly more cautious outlook.”

  • Schwartz emphasized capital deployment and shareholder returns alongside a cost program: “With approximately $2 billion in liquidity, we intend to pursue meaningful M&A, while returning our free cash flow to shareholders through opportunistic share repurchase and dividends,” and said the company was “on track to deliver the remaining $25 million of our $60 million cost-out program by year-end.”

  • Schwartz highlighted two post-quarter-end acquisitions and a new integration role. On MoistureShield, he said it “meaningfully expands our capacity,” “eliminates the need to spend capital on a new greenfield,” and “gives us the needed footprint to double our wood/plastic composite decking manufacturing capacity by 2027.” On leadership, he said Patrick Benton will move to a “newly created Executive Vice President of Operations Integration position.”

  • Quote (CFO & Treasurer Michael Cole): “Adjusted EBITDA was $111 million, down $31 million year-over-year, and adjusted EBITDA margin was 7.6% compared with 8.9% in the prior year period.”

Outlook

  • Cole said the company expects 2026 conditions to remain difficult and signaled results trending to the low end of prior expectations: “We expect the current market environment to persist through 2026,” and “demand for the balance of the year is trending toward the lower end of our prior guidance.”

  • Cole added that cost inflation may take time to recover in earnings: “We expect continued pressure from energy and transportation,” and “the benefit is expected to take time to flow through the income statement this year.”

  • On growth offsets, Schwartz reiterated a major retail initiative despite macro uncertainty: “We continue to expect $100 million of incremental Deckorators growth this year.”

Financial Results

  • Schwartz reported “net sales of $1.46 billion were down 8% from Q1 of 2025,” with declines “representing a 7% decrease in units and a 1% decrease in price,” and said “earnings per share for the quarter was $0.89.”

  • Cole attributed the year-over-year EBITDA decline primarily to Site Built and higher benefit/freight costs: “The decline was driven primarily by Site Built, where gross profit decreased by nearly $19 million, along with higher health care and transportation costs across the portfolio, which increased approximately $7 million and $3 million, respectively.”

  • Cole said liquidity remained strong while working capital rose seasonally: “At the end of March, the company had $714 million in surplus cash and no borrowings under its credit agreements for a total liquidity of approximately $2 billion,” and noted the surplus cash decline was “driven by a typical seasonal working capital build that we expect to convert to cash by early Q4.”

  • Cole provided updated 2026 capital allocation markers, including a lower CapEx plan after MoistureShield: “We currently expect $250 million to $275 million of CapEx, about $50 million lower than our February target due to the MoistureShield transaction,” and said the board approved “a quarterly dividend of $0.36 per share, a 3% increase from a year ago.”

Q&A

  • Kurt Yinger, D.A. Davidson & Co.: asked whether ProWood volumes improve after weather impacts; CEO Schwartz answered, “If you take some of that noise out, it really matches up well to some of the guidance we’ve talked about for single-digit down.”

  • Yinger, D.A. Davidson & Co.: asked whether Buffalo capacity supports the $100 million Deckorators target in Q2 or later; Schwartz said Buffalo shipped “our first truck mid-April,” but added, “as you get to Q3, Q4, we’ll be able to capitalize on a lot of backlog of orders.”

  • Yinger, D.A. Davidson & Co.: asked about fuel/energy headwinds and pass-through timing; Schwartz said, “Most of those are already in place at this point,” and “by the back half of the year for certain, I wouldn’t expect to be taking hit as a result of those increased fuel costs.”

  • W. Andrew Carter, Stifel: asked to size the fuel headwind and lag; Cole answered, “it was about a $3 million headwind in March,” and said pass-through is starting “through freight surcharges and price increases,” expecting completion “in pretty short order in Q2.”

  • Jeffrey Stevenson, Loop Capital: asked how MoistureShield fits Deckorators and distribution; Schwartz said, “We needed additional capacity,” called CoolDeck “extremely exciting,” and said the added distribution partners are “extremely valuable.”

  • Carter, Stifel / Reuben Garner, Benchmark: asked about brand strategy; Schwartz said, “The intent is to run the MoistureShield brand for the remainder of the year and in 2027, we’ll start to transition moving that under the Deckorators umbrella.”

  • Garner, Benchmark: asked for decking mix and capacity; Cole said 2025 decking/railing sales were “about $245 million,” with “about $90 million” Surestone decking and “about $75 million” wood/plastic composite, and added capacity plans moving Surestone to “$350 million of capacity” and wood/plastic composite from “$100 million to $200 million.”

  • Ketan Mamtora, BMO Capital Markets: asked about Site Built pricing pressure and capital allocation; Schwartz said Site Built “continues to be a very pressured market for us on the margin side,” and on M&A added, “the pipeline is the best we’ve had in 5-plus years.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, concentrating on weather-driven demand, the magnitude and timing of fuel/transport inflation pass-through, Deckorators capacity timing versus the $100 million growth target, and ongoing Site Built competitive pressure.

  • Management was slightly positive in strategy but more cautious on macro, with Schwartz acknowledging “a slightly more cautious outlook,” while repeatedly emphasizing confidence in Deckorators growth and actions to offset inflation through surcharges/repricing.

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s “cautiously optimistic” framing for 2026, management’s language shifted toward more near-term caution tied to “ongoing geopolitical uncertainty” and “broadening inflation,” while keeping the same long-term operating targets and Deckorators growth emphasis.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current call added concrete post-quarter actions versus Q4’s forward setup: MoistureShield and Berry Pallets acquisitions, and an operations integration role (Patrick Benton) to “move faster from close to contribution.”

  • Guidance language became more guarded: Q4 described being “cautiously optimistic for 2026,” while Q1 said demand is “trending toward the lower end of our prior guidance” and fuel/transport pressure will “take time to flow through the income statement this year.”

  • Analysts remained heavily focused on Deckorators in both quarters, but Q1 questions leaned more toward capacity ramp timing (Buffalo start, backlog conversion) and inflation pass-through mechanics, reflecting the March cost shock described in prepared remarks.

Risks and Concerns

  • Schwartz cited “competitive pressures,” “geopolitical uncertainty,” and “broadening inflation, particularly around higher transportation costs,” and Cole said input costs could remain pressured by “energy and transportation.”

  • Management characterized Site Built as the most challenging area to reprice, with Schwartz saying cost pass-through is “hardest to pass along” in that business.

  • Integration execution risk was implicitly addressed by the creation of an operations integration leader, with Schwartz stating the goal is ensuring acquisitions “perform to its full potential.”

Final Takeaway

Management described a Q1 shaped by weather disruption, weaker demand, and a March spike in transportation costs, while keeping the company’s capital allocation posture aggressive: deploying roughly $2 billion of liquidity toward M&A and returning cash via dividends and buybacks. The clearest near-term operating focus remained Deckorators—supported by new capacity coming online, MoistureShield’s added composite footprint and CoolDeck technology, and an reiterated expectation of $100 million of incremental growth in 2026—while Site Built was repeatedly framed as the most margin-pressured business amid competitive pricing and inflation pass-through lags.

data.title: Oil finishes lower after prices pushed to four-year high on war escalation worries
data.description:
Crude oil futures pulled back Thursday after Brent briefly topped $126/bbl—its highest since March 2022, shortly after Russia invaded Ukraine - on rising worries over a prolonged conflict between the U.S. and Iran while just a trickle of oil gets through the Strait of Hormuz.

The surge in prices followed reports that President Trump would be briefed by military commanders on possible action against Iran, while Iran vowed not to give up its nuclear or missile technologies and signaled it would maintain control of the strait.

Brent crude (CO1:COM) for June delivery fell 3.4% to go off the board at $114.01/bbl, snapping an eight-session winning streak, bringing it closer to the $110.40/bbl for the July contract.

Oil markets are currently in backwardation—where near-term contracts are typically priced higher than those further out—and as July contracts have been priced well below June, the switch appears to account for much of the significant price shift.

"On top of the expiration liquidation flush today, Brent also came under pressure after European Union economic data came in on the soft side," Mizuho's Robert Yawger said in a note.

ConocoPhillips (COP) CFO Andy O'Brien warned of imminent "critical shortages" of oil for some countries, with the supply pinch likely to significantly worsen as soon as June, Bloomberg reported.

"The markets sort of had a bit of a grace period initially when the tankers that left the Persian Gulf in late February were still on the water; now all of those have reached their destination," O'Brien reportedly said on the company's earnings conference call. "We are going to start to see some import-dependent countries potentially start to face critical shortages as we get into the June-July time frame."

In the U.S., front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for June delivery closed down 1.7% to $105.07/bbl, breaking a three-session winning streak, and front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) for June delivery gained 4.5% to $2.767/MMBtu.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Riot outlines AMD lease ramp to $55.6M annualized run rate by exit 2027 as Corsicana plan rises to 756 MW
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Riot Platforms (RIOT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "The first quarter of 2026 was a definitive inflection point in Riot's transition into one of the most significant and capable data center operators in the industry." (CEO & Executive Director Jason Les)
  • "AMD officially exercised a 25-megawatt expansion option, bringing their total contracted footprint at our Rockdale facility to 50 megawatts" and "initial data center capacity for this expansion will be delivered beginning in November of this year." (CEO & Executive Director Les)
  • "We have initiated development of our first core and shell building using our enhanced 168-megawatt standard design" and "our total planned campus capacity now stands at 756 megawatts of critical IT capacity." (CEO & Executive Director Les)
  • "Importantly, we did not issue any common equity during the quarter." (CFO, Executive VP and Head of Corporate Development & Strategy Jason Chung)

Outlook

  • "We anticipate exiting 2026 with an annualized operating lease revenue run rate of $37.8 million, scaling to a run rate of $55.6 million as we exit 2027 and AMD's full 50-megawatt footprint comes online." (CEO & Executive Director Jason Les)
  • "Phase 3 highlighted in blue will deliver 10 megawatts in November of 2026" and "Phase 4 highlighted in orange represents the remaining 15 megawatts for delivery in May of 2027." (CEO & Executive Director Les)
  • "We continue to target attractive loan-to-cost ratios in the range of 80%" for "project level, non-recourse financing structures for the AMD lease." (CFO Chung)

Financial Results

  • "For the first quarter of 2026, Riot reported total revenue of $167 million" and "we recorded a GAAP net loss of $500 million, or $1.44 per diluted share and an adjusted EBITDA loss of $311 million." (CFO, Executive VP and Head of Corporate Development & Strategy Jason Chung)
  • "This loss was driven by non-cash mark-to-market accounting adjustments on our Bitcoin holdings of $326.7 million and non-cash depreciation and amortization expense of $97.7 million." (CFO Chung)
  • "Riot produced 1,473 Bitcoin in the first quarter" and "ended the quarter with a deployed hash rate of 42.5 exahash." (CFO Chung)
  • "We generated $21 million in power curtailment credits, driving our net cost of power down to $0.03 per kilowatt hour" and "lowering our direct cost to mine Bitcoin to $44,629 per Bitcoin." (CFO Chung)
  • "In our newly added Data Center segment, we successfully exited the quarter with 5 megawatts of critical IT capacity fully online and generated $33.2 million in total revenue, consisting of $900,000 in operating lease revenue and $32.2 million in tenant fit-out services revenue." (CFO Chung)

Q&A

  • Paul Golding, Macquarie Research: "I was wondering if you could just give some color on those puts and takes" on the AMD expansion economics; CEO Les: "it is the same rental rate in terms" and "the only economic difference really is that lower build-out cost" because "we're leveraging the full building preparation that was already done in the original phase." (CEO & Executive Director Les)
  • Paul Golding, Macquarie Research: asked about timeline and the ROFR conversion; CEO Les: "this is a pretty fast time line" and "we converted the ROFR to an option really just to simplify the pathway of expansion of the lease of AMD." (CEO & Executive Director Les)
  • John Todaro, Needham & Company: asked for an update on non-AMD leasing; CEO Les: "those discussions have advanced considerably since then" and "there are no gating items or issues here." (CEO & Executive Director Les)
  • John Todaro, Needham & Company: asked about broader demand signals; CEO Les: "we see the broader theme of data center demand outpacing supply continuing" and "each buyer is always in a different phase of their own buying cycle." (CEO & Executive Director Les)
  • Mike Grondahl, Northland Capital Markets: asked about data center revenue and margins; CFO Chung: "$32.2 million relates directly to tenant fit-out services" and "the margin on the core operating lease component for this quarter was 91%" with expectations that "will drive NOI margins towards that targeted 80% range." (CFO Chung)
  • Stephen Glagola, KBW: asked about leadership changes and leasing; CEO Les: "these changes have had absolutely no impact on development or commercial discussions" and "we have an extremely strong team in place to execute at both Rockdale and Corsicana concurrently." (CEO & Executive Director Les)
  • Brett Knoblauch, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked if Corsicana interest is still for the full site and about procurement; CEO Les: "yes, that still remains the case" and "we previously secured and have actually already begun receiving the necessary substation equipment." (CEO & Executive Director Les)
  • Brian Dobson, Clear Street: asked whether Bitcoin sales continue and views on debt; CFO Chung: "our Bitcoin treasury and operating cash flows remain the most capital-efficient, non-dilutive sources of funding" and "we're evaluating a wide spectrum of debt options ranging from asset-specific project financing to broader corporate debt markets." (CFO Chung)
  • Henry Hearle: asked about AMD option cadence/expiry and what "power availability" means; CEO Les: "that's really going to be driven by them" and "there's some confidentiality to this agreement." (CEO & Executive Director Les)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive but detail-oriented, repeatedly pressing on AMD economics, the ROFR-to-option change, leasing timelines beyond AMD, margin normalization, and financing sources, including "Do you expect that to continue?" and "would you elaborate on your view of, call it, long-term debt financing." (Brian Dobson, Clear Street LLC)
  • Management tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks and largely steady in Q&A, using categorical phrasing such as "there are no gating items or issues here" and "these changes have had absolutely no impact" while also limiting disclosures with "there's some confidentiality to this agreement." (CEO & Executive Director Jason Les)
  • Versus last quarter, management’s language shifted from positioning and validation to execution and scaling, moving from "Today, I am exceptionally proud to share the significant milestones we have achieved" (CEO & Executive Director Jason Les, Q4 2025) to "a definitive inflection point" and explicit ramp metrics tied to AMD’s phased deliveries. (CEO & Executive Director Les, Q1 2026)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • AMD expanded from the prior-quarter framing of "a 10-year agreement" for an initial "25 megawatts" with "an option to expand by an additional 75 megawatts" and "a right of first refusal on an additional 100 megawatts" (CEO & Executive Director Jason Les, Q4 2025) to the current-quarter update that "AMD now has 50 megawatts of critical IT capacity under contract" and "they now hold an additional 100-megawatt option, which replaces their prior right of first refusal." (CEO & Executive Director Les, Q1 2026)
  • Corsicana plans moved from "the development of our first core and shell at Corsicana" and early site-scale discussion in Q4 2025 (CEO & Executive Director Jason Les) to a redesigned standard where "we have consolidated the 2 buildings we previously announced into a single larger building with 168 megawatts of critical IT capacity" and "our total planned campus capacity now stands at 756 megawatts of critical IT capacity." (CEO & Executive Director Les, Q1 2026)
  • Financing messaging remained consistent on avoiding equity issuance, shifting from "our funding hierarchy... starts with the Bitcoin treasury" (CFO Jason Chung, Q4 2025) to Q1 2026 emphasis that "we did not issue any common equity during the quarter" alongside a more detailed "lease, finance, build and recycle" capital-cycle description. (CFO Chung, Q1 2026)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Leasing this type of capacity to top-tier tenants, that is an enormous lift" and "that can have an unpredictable time line." (CEO & Executive Director Jason Les)
  • "While I can't really comment on the specific spreads at this point" on AMD financing, management cited "initial feedback has been really positive" and tied the approach to "project level, non-recourse financing structures" supported by contracted cash flows. (CFO Jason Chung)
  • "These are components that are essential for data center development" and "lead times are lengthening" for constrained equipment; management positioned mitigation in vertical integration, stating, "Because Riot owns a dedicated switchgear and power distribution manufacturer, we can sequence, prioritize and derisk the schedule critical equipment." (CFO Chung)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as an inflection toward being an active data center operator, highlighted AMD’s expansion to 50 MW at Rockdale with phased deliveries into 2027, and laid out a visible operating-lease revenue ramp to a $55.6 million annualized run rate by exit 2027. The quarter added a new Data Center segment reporting line with early revenue dominated by reimbursed fit-out services and recurring lease revenue at high initial margins, alongside continued low-cost Bitcoin mining supported by curtailment credits. Across Q&A, management emphasized non-dilutive funding via operating cash flow and Bitcoin sales, pursuit of tenant-backed project finance, and ongoing—but timeline-uncertain—leasing discussions beyond AMD at both Rockdale and Corsicana.

data.title: Blue Owl targets 58.5% 2026 FRE margin while expecting to hit $7.5B net lease fund hard cap by year-end
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “We raised $57 billion of capital over the last 12 months… and $11 billion in the first quarter,” and “revenues increased by 13%, fee-related earnings by 14% and distributable earnings by 11% compared to the first quarter of 2025,” Marc S. Lipschultz (Co-Founder, Co-CEO Marc Lipschultz) said.
  • “Direct lending represents only 37% of Blue Owl’s AUM… real assets is now 27% of AUM and GP Strategic Capital is 22%,” Marc Lipschultz said, adding, “nearly 3/4 of equity capital we’ve raised over the last 12 months has been outside of direct lending.”
  • “The actual impact to Blue Owl’s revenues and earnings for the first quarter was quite modest,” Marc Lipschultz said on nontraded BDC flows, citing “net outflows of roughly $170 million from OCIC and OTIC,” and adding that “these 2 funds collectively comprise less than 17% of our total AUM.”
  • “This marks the fourth data center project above $10 billion announced in less than 18 months for which Blue Owl will play a critical role,” Marc Lipschultz said, referencing “Amazon announced a $12 billion data center campus with investment for Blue Owl’s digital infrastructure funds.”
  • “We modestly increased our FRE margin, expanding to 58.4% for the quarter,” Alan Kirshenbaum (Chief Financial Officer Alan Kirshenbaum) said, and “AUM not yet paying fees increased to $30 billion, representing approximately $350 million of expected annual management fees once deployed.”

Outlook

  • “We remain focused on disciplined expense management,” Alan Kirshenbaum said, and “continue to see a path to achieve our goal of 58.5% FRE margins for 2026.”
  • “We remain committed to paying out our $0.92 dividend for 2026,” Alan Kirshenbaum said.
  • “We… continue to expect to hit our hard cap of $7.5 billion by the end of this year,” Alan Kirshenbaum said about the latest net lease flagship.
  • Compared with last quarter’s language, Alan Kirshenbaum had said, “we believe we can see modest margin expansion for 2026, targeting approximately 58.5% FRE margin,” and added in Q4 that “we believe we can show a modest increase in the growth rate for FRE per share, and we feel we can accelerate that growth in 2027 versus 2026.”

Financial results

  • “Reporting fee-related earnings, or FRE of $0.25 per share and distributable earnings or DE of $0.19 per share,” Ann Dai (MD & Head of Investor Relations Ann Dai) said, and “we declared a dividend of $0.23 per share for the first quarter.”
  • “Management fees were up 13%,” Alan Kirshenbaum said, and “equity capital raised grew by 35%” versus the year-ago quarter.
  • “Repayments in the portfolio were $6.4 billion for the first quarter,” Alan Kirshenbaum said, while “spreads at least 50 basis points wider” were “beginning” to show up in the origination pipeline.
  • “Catch-up fees in the credit business… was about $7 million for our BOSE products,” Alan Kirshenbaum said.

Q&A

  • Craig Siegenthaler, BofA Securities: “help us size the credit inflows” and “what specific funds” Alan Kirshenbaum: “about $1 billion… into our non-traded BDCs” and “ASOF IX… final close… Alt credit continues to grow.”
  • William Katz, TD Cowen: “what you’re hearing from the gatekeepers” on redemptions/flows Alan Kirshenbaum: “financial advisers… want the products to work as designed, 5% tenders per quarter,” and “paydowns in OCIC were almost $3 billion… versus… gross redemptions at $1 billion.”
  • Brennan Hawken, BMO: fee-rate drivers Alan Kirshenbaum: “we did have some BOSE one-time catch-up fees,” and flagged “ASOF IX… continues to grow.”
  • Michael Brown, UBS: deployment timing and sector focus Marc Lipschultz: “we like them at higher spreads,” and “it’s safe to say as today, we are working down our exposure to software given the level of uncertainty.”
  • Glenn Schorr, Evercore ISI: portfolio health vs headlines; software maturity wall Marc Lipschultz: “we have seen no material negative developments… no material change in watch list… nonaccruals,” and “our average principal recovery… has been $0.80 on the dollar.”
  • Brian McKenna, Citizens JMP: SpaceX exposure and offsetting losses Marc Lipschultz: “we made about 10x our money,” and “we’ve sold about half of it at a $1.25 trillion valuation.”
  • Steven Chubak, Wolfe: 58.5% margin with softer retail Alan Kirshenbaum: “we have levers… to make sure… we expect to continue to maintain that 58.5% FRE margin.”
  • Patrick Davitt, Autonomous: FRE growth amid retail flow changes Alan Kirshenbaum: “there could be a wider range of outcomes for revenues,” and “we think we can beat those numbers for 2026.”
  • Wilma Burdis, Raymond James: software LTV update Alan Kirshenbaum: “LTVs came from low 30s to low 40s,” and Marc Lipschultz: “that’s about 60%” equity cushion.
  • Crispin Love, Piper Sandler: channel outlook and cadence Alan Kirshenbaum: “near-term softness… in the non-traded BDCs and wealth,” while “BOSE… closed at approximately $3 billion” and “ASOF IX… closed at approximately $3 billion.”
  • Kenneth Worthington, JPMorgan: direct lending fee-paying AUM Alan Kirshenbaum: “I would expect… fee-paying AUM grow… for direct lending specifically.”
  • Benjamin Budish, Barclays: comp trajectory Alan Kirshenbaum: “stock-based comp… $365 million was my guidance.”
  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: revolver increase; dividend coverage Alan Kirshenbaum: “We make our TRA payment. We pay bonuses in 1Q,” and “we’re committed to paying the dividend of $0.92 for 2026.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, pressing on “redemptions,” “software maturity wall,” “not fully covered” dividend optics, and fee-rate “noise,” including Glenn Schorr’s framing that “people are looking for the public markets crush the equities.”
  • Management tone was slightly positive and data-driven, repeatedly emphasizing “facts” and portfolio stability, including Marc Lipschultz saying, “answer those questions with facts,” and “we have seen no material negative developments in our portfolios.”
  • Versus last quarter, Q4 management also leaned into “facts” against “noise,” while Q1 added more macro caution, with Marc Lipschultz saying, “there’s certainly more uncertainty in the macro and geopolitical landscape.”

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 fundraising was framed as “$11 billion raised,” while Q4 highlighted “over $17 billion during the fourth quarter” and “we raised $56 billion of capital across the business” for 2025.
  • Q1 commentary broadened the diversification narrative with “direct lending represents only 37%” of AUM, while Q4 emphasized direct lending credit quality and addressed “software AI disruption” as an “emerged… big theme.”
  • Q1’s private-wealth discussion centered on modest impact and concentration of tenders, with “net outflows of roughly $170 million,” while Q4 described an industry “slowdown… and elevated redemptions” but also “a record quarter for equity raised in private wealth.”

Risks and concerns

  • “Geopolitical uncertainty” and “interest rate volatility” were cited by Marc Lipschultz alongside “increased attention to private credit.”
  • “Elevated industry-wide redemption requests” were acknowledged by Marc Lipschultz, while Alan Kirshenbaum emphasized structure and liquidity, saying “paydowns in OCIC were almost $3 billion… versus… gross redemptions at $1 billion.”
  • On software/AI uncertainty and refinancing, Marc Lipschultz said, “there is a software maturity wall coming in 2028 and ’29,” and added, “it’s safe to say as today, we are working down our exposure to software given the level of uncertainty.”

Final takeaway

Management emphasized platform diversification and deployment opportunities alongside a commitment to a $0.92 2026 dividend and a 58.5% 2026 FRE margin target, while positioning net outflows in nontraded BDCs as modest relative to total AUM. Leadership highlighted ongoing fundraising breadth, $30 billion of AUM not yet paying fees as embedded management-fee growth, spread widening in credit, and large real-asset pipelines, while repeatedly asserting that direct lending portfolio indicators such as watch list and nonaccruals showed “no material” deterioration despite elevated scrutiny of private credit and software-related uncertainty.

data.title: National MI signals 2026 volume may track 2025 as rates shift after a $12.3B NIW quarter
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NMI Holdings (NMIH) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "I'm pleased to report that in the first quarter, National MI again delivered standout operating performance, continued growth in our insured portfolio and strong financial results." (Executive Chairman Bradley Shuster)

  • "In the first quarter, we generated $12.3 billion of NIW volume. Ending the period with a record $222.3 billion of high-quality, high-performing, primary insurance-in-force." (Executive Chairman Shuster)

  • "Notwithstanding these strong positives, however, macro risks do remain and we've maintained a proactive stance with respect to our pricing, risk selection and reinsurance decisioning." (President, CEO & Director Adam Pollitzer)

  • "Total revenue in the first quarter was a record $183.5 million, and we delivered adjusted net income of $99.4 million or $1.28 per diluted share and a 15.2% adjusted return on equity." (President, CEO & Director Pollitzer)

  • "12-month persistency was 82.2% in the first quarter compared to 83.4% in the fourth quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Aurora Swithenbank)

  • "In the first quarter, we repurchased $27.7 million of common stock, retiring 716,000 shares at an average price of $38.65." (Executive VP & CFO Swithenbank)

Outlook

  • "As we look out across the year, we don't provide guidance, but I'll trace back to some comments that I made on our Q4 call. Coming into the year, we generally expected that 2026 volume would look similar to how 2025 volume trended from an overall market standpoint." (President, CEO & Director Pollitzer)

  • "Now that they've sold off as we look ahead through the remainder of the year, I think we're still calibrating off of 2025 performance." (President, CEO & Director Pollitzer)

  • "We will expect increases over time, but we try to be very disciplined about minimizing those increases." (Executive VP & CFO Swithenbank)

Financial Results

  • "Net premiums earned in the first quarter were a record $154.8 million compared to $152.5 million in the fourth quarter and $149.4 million in the first quarter of 2025." (Executive VP & CFO Swithenbank)

  • "Investment income was $28.6 million in the first quarter compared to $27.5 million in the fourth quarter and $23.7 million in the first quarter of 2025." (Executive VP & CFO Swithenbank)

  • "We had 8,044 defaults at March 31, compared to 7,661 at December 31, and our default rate was 1.17% at quarter end." (Executive VP & CFO Swithenbank)

  • "Claims expense in the first quarter were $20.7 million compared to $21.2 million in the fourth quarter and $4.5 million in the first quarter of 2025." (Executive VP & CFO Swithenbank)

  • "At quarter end, we reported $3.6 billion of total available assets under PMIERs and $2.2 billion of risk-based required assets. Excess available assets were $1.5 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Swithenbank)

Q&A

  • Bose George, KBW: Asked about reserve per new notice vs. last quarter. Executive VP & CFO Swithenbank: "It's $14,200, which is broadly consistent with the $14,500 that we established last quarter."

  • Terry Ma, Barclays: Asked whether credit normalization is accelerating and about macro risks (including higher energy prices). President, CEO & Director Pollitzer: "There's nothing that stood out this quarter that makes us think we will get to normal quicker than where we were otherwise pacing" and "in terms of gas prices themselves, we really don't expect to see a notable impact."

  • Richard Shane, JPMorgan: Asked how shifting confidence and rates affected volume. President, CEO & Director Pollitzer: "The period you talked about January and February, we saw a continued rally in rates" and "since then, rates have sold off" and "that's going to be a much more significant driver than the psychology and confidence that comes around what's happening in the Middle East."

  • Mark Hughes, Truist: Asked about competition and what it implies for the year. President, CEO & Director Pollitzer: "We see a broadly balanced and constructive market environment" and "we don't provide guidance" and "we generally expected that 2026 volume would look similar to how 2025 volume trended."

  • Mihir Bhatia, BofA Securities: Asked about higher in-period losses, reserve dynamics, NIW down quarter-over-quarter, and reinsurance profit commission trends. President, CEO & Director Pollitzer: "We're actually going out and modeling each individual default" and "those post-COVID vintages, the '22s and later... inherently have less embedded equity in them." Executive VP & CFO Swithenbank: "If you look at what's driving that change, it really is the larger loan size of the loans that are in default" and on profit commission "that's -- you put your finger on it."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, pressing on loss normalization and reserve dynamics, including "anything that may suggest that the rate of credit normalization may be accelerating" (Terry Ma, Barclays) and questioning why losses rose year-over-year (Mihir Bhatia, BofA Securities).

  • Management was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more neutral in Q&A, emphasizing resilience and limited near-term concern: "we were really encouraged by the performance. Nothing stood out to us that we highlight as a point of concern." (President, CEO & Director Pollitzer)

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management kept similar framing on discipline and resilience, repeating "balanced and constructive" market language, while Q1 added more rate-path and geopolitical context.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q1, National MI reported "$12.3 billion of NIW" (Executive Chairman Shuster) versus Q4 "$14.2 billion of NIW" (President, CEO & Director Pollitzer), alongside higher insurance-in-force of "$222.3 billion" (Executive Chairman Shuster) versus "$221.4 billion" in Q4 (President, CEO & Director Pollitzer).

  • Persistency declined sequentially, with Q1 "82.2%" (Executive VP & CFO Swithenbank) versus Q4 "83.4%" (Executive VP & CFO Swithenbank).

  • Q4 highlighted reinsurance execution and forward-flow coverage, stating, "in the fourth quarter, we entered into a series of new quota share and excess of loss reinsurance treaties... provide us with forward flow coverage for all new business produced through 2028" (Executive VP & CFO Swithenbank), while Q1 discussion centered more on seasoning, post-COVID vintages, and reserve drivers.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Real risks do remain" and the company said it has "maintained a proactive stance with respect to our pricing, risk selection and reinsurance decisioning." (President, CEO & Director Pollitzer)

  • Management flagged credit mix and severity sensitivity to newer vintages: "more and more of our ultimate claims... are from those post-COVID vintages, the '22s and later, which inherently have less embedded equity in them." (Executive VP & CFO Swithenbank)

  • Analysts concentrated on whether normalization is accelerating and what drives reserve increases, and management responded that quarter-to-quarter movements did not present "a point of concern" in observed credit performance. (President, CEO & Director Pollitzer)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a record-revenue quarter with strong profitability and continued insurance-in-force growth, while positioning 2026 market volume expectations as broadly similar to 2025 even as mortgage rates moved higher late in the quarter. In Q&A, analysts repeatedly tested whether credit normalization and losses are accelerating, and management attributed reserve and loss movements to loan size, seasoning, and greater exposure to post-COVID vintages with less embedded equity, while maintaining that nothing in current credit trends stood out as a new concern.

data.title: FMC targets ~$1B 2026 debt paydown while keeping 2026 sales guidance at $3.6B-$3.8B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: FMC Corporation (FMC) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "During the first quarter, we delivered results that exceeded the midpoint of our guidance range." (President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Pierre Brondeau)

  • "We are continuing to target approximately $1 billion of debt paydown during 2026" and "expect to sign a definitive agreement in May" for "the sale of our India commercial business." (President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau)

  • "We intend to shift production from high-cost plants to lower-cost sources in Asia" and "expect this transition will be completed by Q1 2027." (President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau)

  • "We continue to advance our post-patent strategy" for Rynaxypyr, including "positive reactions to a price repositioning" with "strong volume growth for high load formulations and differentiated mixtures." (President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau)

  • "Sales of these products doubled year-over-year in the first quarter" and "we recently received regulatory approval for Isoflex active in the EU." (President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau)

  • "First quarter sales of $762 million" and "first quarter EBITDA of $72 million" and "adjusted loss per share of $0.23." (President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau)

  • "We ended the first quarter with gross debt of approximately $4.5 billion" and "net debt of approximately $4.1 billion" and "gross debt to trailing 12-month EBITDA was 5.7x." (Executive VP & CFO Andrew Sandifer)

Outlook

  • "We expect second quarter revenue to be between $850 million and $900 million" and "adjusted earnings per share is expected to be between $0.16 and $0.26." (President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau)

  • "Our full year 2026 financial guidance ranges are unchanged from our last call" with "sales of $3.6 billion to $3.8 billion" and "EBITDA is expected to be $670 million to $730 million" and "adjusted EPS is expected to be $1.63 to $1.89." (President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau)

  • "We are maintaining our full year guidance despite the increased uncertainty related to tariffs and the conflict in Iran" and "we are currently assuming that the Iran-related cost pressure and tariff-related benefits largely offset each other." (President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau)

Financial results

  • "First quarter sales of $762 million" with "sales were 4% lower than prior year" and "sales were up 1% on a like-for-like basis after excluding India"; management also said "volume grew 2% and FX was a 5% tailwind." (President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau)

  • "First quarter interest expense of $64.8 million was up $14.7 million" and "we continue to expect full year 2026 interest expense to be in the range of $255 million to $275 million." (Executive VP & CFO Sandifer)

  • "Free cash flow in the first quarter was negative $628 million" and "we continue to expect free cash flow for 2026 to be in the range of negative $65 million to positive $65 million." (Executive VP & CFO Sandifer)

Q&A

  • Michael Sison, Wells Fargo: asked about "biggest challenges" to the H2 ramp and the Brazil sales org; President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau said H2 improvement is expected to be driven by "non-diamide core" at "$150 million to $200 million," Rynaxypyr at "$50 million to $80 million," and "new active ingredients" at "$175 million to $200 million," adding, "we already have orders representing 32% of our H2 direct sales in Brazil."

  • Patrick Fischer, Goldman Sachs: pressed on Rynaxypyr partner sales math; President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau said 2025 Rynaxypyr was "about $800 million" split "$600 million of branded sales" and "about $200 million of partner sales," while in 2026 FMC is "forecasting $700 million" with "$600 million of branded Rynaxypyr" and "partner sales decreasing to a number lower than $100 million."

  • Joshua Spector, UBS: asked about Iran-related input costs and whether generics pricing is a risk/opportunity; President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau said "we have not factored anything in the forecast" and "at this stage, we're looking at the impact being pretty muted."

  • Vincent Andrews, Morgan Stanley: asked what else could be monetized and potential proceeds; President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau said FMC has "about line of sight to $700 million, about 70% of our target" from items "in active negotiation and well advanced."

  • Jeffrey Zekauskas, JPMorgan: asked price dynamics excluding diamides and Cyazypyr pricing; Executive VP & CFO Sandifer said "for the non-diamide products, prices were down in the low single digits percent" and "for Cyazypyr, prices were relatively flat."

  • Matthew DeYoe, BofA: asked about tariff refunds and Rynaxypyr upside/downside; President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau said on tariffs, "Applying doesn't mean you get it" and "we do not know," and on Rynaxypyr, "the real test is in the second half of the year."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative tone centered on forecast credibility and mechanics, including "the math doesn't triangulate for me" (Patrick Fischer, Goldman Sachs) and probing for downside framing on H2 Rynaxypyr (Matthew DeYoe, BofA).

  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks with repeated confidence language including "we are seeing early signals that give us confidence" and "we are weeks, maybe days away from signing an agreement" (President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau), while responses on tariffs/inputs emphasized uncertainty: "we do not know" (President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau).

  • Versus last quarter: both calls kept full-year ranges intact, but Q1 introduced more explicit uncertainty language tied to "tariffs" and "the conflict in Iran" alongside an expectation to "provide an updated outlook" next call (President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Strategic posture stayed consistent: Q4 announced the Board "authorized exploring strategic options" and Q1 reiterated that "evaluation of strategic alternatives announced in February 2026 is progressing" (President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau).

  • Timing became more specific on debt actions: Q4 said India binding bids were expected in Q2, while Q1 said FMC expects to "sign a definitive agreement in May" and added "advanced discussions" for licensing with an upfront payment expected "in the coming weeks" (President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau).

  • Balance sheet leverage tightened sequentially: Q4 ended with "net debt of approximately $3.5 billion" and Q1 reported "net debt of approximately $4.1 billion" tied to "our normal seasonal working capital build" (Executive VP & CFO Sandifer).

  • Analyst focus shifted from strategic review mechanics (Q4) toward execution risk in H2, partner-sales drag, and Iran/tariff sensitivity (Q1).

Risks and concerns

  • "Challenging margins and stressed liquidity for customers and growers" and "increased the willingness to use generic products or skip some preventative applications." (President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau)

  • "Increased uncertainty related to tariffs and the conflict in Iran" with "higher energy, transportation and petrochemical costs" beginning to flow into product costs. (President, CEO & Non-Executive Chairman Brondeau)

  • "We intend to go to market this quarter with a secured high-yield bond offering to redeem $500 million of notes that mature in October, market conditions permitting." (Executive VP & CFO Sandifer)

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 as ahead of its internal guideposts while keeping full-year 2026 ranges unchanged, with the near-term stock-moving catalysts centered on (1) a targeted ~$1B 2026 debt paydown—led by an India commercial-business sale expected to reach a definitive agreement in May plus licensing/asset monetizations, (2) the post-patent Rynaxypyr transition that depends heavily on mix shifting toward higher-end formulations in Q3/Q4, and (3) external cost and tariff uncertainty tied to Iran and U.S. trade actions that management said it will revisit with updated outlook detail at the next earnings call.

data.title: GoDaddy outlines $5.195B-$5.275B FY2026 revenue outlook while targeting ~33% Q2 normalized EBITDA margin
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: GoDaddy (GDDY) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered revenue growth of 6%." (CEO & Director Amanpal Bhutani)
  • "This new Airo AI Builder product offering has rapidly scaled to $10 million plus in annualized bookings run rate within weeks of its beta launch." (CEO & Director Bhutani)
  • "We signed a couple of partnerships over the last quarter with real-world use cases." (CEO & Director Bhutani)
  • "Airo Care is now rolled out to more than 50 markets and 20 languages." (CEO & Director Bhutani)
  • "We refined the program to better balance customer acquisition and bookings." (CEO & Director Bhutani)
  • "We delivered revenue at the high end of our guidance, while expanding our normalized EBITDA margin by over 200 basis points." (Chief Financial Officer Mark McCaffrey)
  • "We generated strong free cash flow of $474 million, bringing our trailing 12-month free cash flow to $1.68 billion." (CFO McCaffrey)

Outlook

  • "We are reaffirming our full year 2026 guidance provided in February and expect total revenue to be within a range of $5.195 billion to $5.275 billion." (CFO McCaffrey)
  • "For Q2, we are targeting total revenue of $1.285 billion to $1.305 billion." (CFO McCaffrey)
  • "For Q2, we are projecting a normalized EBITDA margin of approximately 33%, and we are reaffirming our target of over 33% for the full year." (CFO McCaffrey)
  • "We reaffirm our full year free cash flow target of approximately $1.8 billion." (CFO McCaffrey)

Financial Results

  • "For the quarter, total revenue grew 6% on both a reported and constant currency basis to $1.3 billion, and ARR grew 6% to $4.3 billion." (CFO McCaffrey)
  • "For our high-margin A&C segment, we drove 12% growth in revenue to $0.5 billion." (CFO McCaffrey)
  • "Our Core Platform segment delivered revenue growth of 3% to $769 million." (CFO McCaffrey)
  • "Total bookings grew 3% to $1.5 billion." (CFO McCaffrey)
  • "Normalized EBITDA grew 13% to $414 million, delivering 210 basis points of margin expansion to 33% and exceeding our guide for the quarter." (CFO McCaffrey)

Q&A

  • Vikram Kesavabhotla, Baird: "how do you ensure that you're attracting the right type of customer"; CEO & Director Bhutani: "our strategy is to attract high-intent customers" and "the way we define high intent is looking at the traffic coming in by channel and then looking at the activation and attach of other products."
  • Hoi-Fung Wong, Oppenheimer: "what this business or this product could potentially grow to"; CEO & Director Bhutani: "it's super early" and "there is a lot to do to get to what we think the long-term run rate can be."
  • Trevor Young, Barclays: "any updated thoughts on capital allocation and buyback appetite"; CFO McCaffrey: "don't look at any particular quarter look at our history" and "buybacks are still a strong way or a strong lever for us to return value to our shareholders."
  • Mark Zgutowicz, Benchmark: "Could you break down the unit economics"; CEO & Director Bhutani: "it's too early to talk about sort of the more detailed pieces" and "we are building a product that... comes at a gross margin and a price point that works for our customer and for GoDaddy."
  • John Halpert, Cantor Fitzgerald: "give us some more color on what exactly that product was"; CEO & Director Bhutani: "we decided to tune them to balance bookings and customer count" and "we took the opportunity to retire an old product"; CFO McCaffrey: "it didn't have any impact on bookings" and "we will continue to evaluate our portfolio."

Sentiment Analysis

  • "it's super early" and "it's too early to talk" appeared repeatedly in analyst-driven follow-ups on AI Builder economics, suggesting a neutral-to-slightly skeptical tone around durability and monetization visibility. (CEO & Director Bhutani)
  • "we are reaffirming" and "we remain focused" dominated management language, with a consistently confident and controlled tone across prepared remarks and Q&A. (CFO McCaffrey)
  • In the prior quarter, management also emphasized early-stage AI monetization, including "Airo.ai is already monetized with growing adoption" and "we will provide greater visibility into the underlying performance metrics" as adoption scales, indicating continuity in message while Q1 added more concrete adoption proof points. (CEO & Director Bhutani)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q4, management framed the domains promotion as a meaningful near-term trade-off: "the promotional price reduced up-front bookings and near-term revenue"; in Q1, management said, "We refined the program to better balance customer acquisition and bookings" and CFO McCaffrey added, "this quarter reflects the peak impact of both these dynamics." (CEO & Director Amanpal Bhutani; CFO McCaffrey)
  • Q4 highlighted an ANS milestone: "MuleSoft, a Salesforce company, and GoDaddy launched an integration"; Q1 emphasized expansion and traction, including "Non-GoDaddy agents in GoDaddy's ANS implementation now number in the thousands." (CEO & Director Bhutani)
  • Analyst focus shifted from promo mechanics and AI cost visibility in Q4 ("how should investors think about the impact to your gross and EBITDA margins") toward AI Builder scale, unit economics, and channel ramp in Q1 ("break down the unit economics" and "how much of the traffic are you exposing"). (Vikram Kesavabhotla, Baird; Mark Zgutowicz, Benchmark; Naved Khan, B. Riley)

Risks and Concerns

  • "softness in non-core GoDaddy hosting" and "tougher compares in aftermarket" were cited as offsets within Core Platform performance. (CFO McCaffrey)
  • "we are thoughtfully monitoring the mix between new and existing products as we scale" highlighted execution risk around product mix and margin discipline as AI Builder marketing ramps. (CEO & Director Bhutani)
  • "Any sort of pricing change would have to wait until we get to parity on the customer experience" signaled timing dependency for future Websites + Marketing monetization actions. (CEO & Director Bhutani)

Final Takeaway

GoDaddy’s management emphasized operating leverage alongside an AI product and operations push, pairing "revenue growth of 6%" with normalized EBITDA margin expansion to 33% and $474 million of free cash flow, while pointing investors to early AI-native product traction including "$10 million plus" annualized bookings run rate for Airo AI Builder, broader Airo Care deployment, and reaffirmed FY2026 targets of $5.195 billion to $5.275 billion in revenue and approximately $1.8 billion in free cash flow.

data.title: Broadstone net lease maintains 2026 AFFO per share guidance of $1.53-$1.57 while advancing $382M build-to-suit pipeline
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Broadstone Net Lease (BNL) Q1 2026

Management view

  • CEO John Moragne said, "After a strong finish to 2025, we carried that momentum into the first quarter of 2026, delivering 5.6% AFFO growth year-over-year." He also highlighted capital deployment and portfolio execution, saying the company "deployed $171.9 million during the quarter" and "addressed nearly half of our 2026 lease maturities with a recapture rate of 119%."

  • Moragne detailed new build-to-suit activity, including "a new state-of-the-art sub-same-day distribution center located in Sarasota, Florida for Amazon" and "a retail development for Academy Sports in Magnolia, Texas," and added that after quarter-end Broadstone "closed on the land and started funding a new presort battery recycling facility for Tesla" near Austin.

  • President Ryan Albano framed strategy around structured transactions, stating, "Our focus is on transactions where thoughtful structuring can materially enhance outcomes, driving higher yields, embedding growth and protecting downside through multiple exit pathways." On the development funnel, he said Broadstone is "actively evaluating approximately $1.3 billion of build-to-suit opportunities" and aims to keep an active committed pipeline "in the $350 million to $500 million range."

  • CFO Kevin Fennell said, "During the quarter, we generated adjusted funds from operations of $76.9 million or $0.38 per share," and added, "With respect to the balance sheet, we ended the quarter with pro forma leverage of 5.8x, unchanged quarter-over-quarter."

Outlook

  • Fennell said, "Lastly, we are maintaining our 2026 per share guidance range of $1.53 to $1.57 with no changes to our key assumptions." He also noted, "Despite no bad debt in the first quarter, we are also maintaining our full year assumption of 75 basis points of lost rent within our 2026 guidance and plan to revisit this assumption throughout the year."

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management’s guidance posture remained unchanged, as Fennell previously said it was "reiterating our 2026 per share guidance range of $1.53 to $1.57 per share" and in Q1 said it is "maintaining" that same range.

Financial results

  • Fennell attributed Q1 drivers as follows: "Results benefited from strong same-store rent growth of 2.8% and from recent investment activity and build-to-suits reaching stabilization," and "notably benefited from no loss rent realized during the quarter and lower nonreimbursable property expenses."

  • On expenses and dividend, Fennell said, "G&A remains well controlled with core expenses totaling $7.8 million during the first quarter," and added, "Last week, our Board of Directors elected to maintain our $0.2925 dividend per share payable to holders of record as of June 30, 2026, on or before July 15."

Q&A

  • Anthony Paolone, JPMorgan: Asked about interim capital commitments for Project Triboro sitework; President Albano replied, "probably for this year, less than $15 million," adding the data-center-specific infrastructure is "being pushed off at this point until we're a little further along in our decision-making process."

  • Eric Borden, BMO: Pressed on zoning timing risk at Triboro and on future ATM usage; CEO Moragne said, "none of this is out of the ordinary" and "it does not change our time line," and on equity issuance said, "opportunistic is the right word to use" and it depends on "share price" and the "opportunity set in the pipeline."

  • Caitlin Burrows, Goldman Sachs: Asked about build-to-suit duration and updates on Claire’s/Red Lobster; Moragne said, "we usually work on an assumption of -- like the average in the pipeline is about 15 months," and on tenant items said, "No announcements to make" on Claire’s and "no material change" on Red Lobster.

  • Jenny (for Ronald Kamdem), Morgan Stanley: Asked why keep the lost-rent assumption despite no Q1 loss rent and asked about Charles River credit; Moragne said, "we'll reevaluate our bad debt assumption at least halfway through the year," and on Charles River said, "we feel very comfortable with the credit relative to our exposure."

  • Zachary Light, BTIG: Asked whether the Charles River transaction was marketed and whether similar structured deals could be replicated; Moragne said, "So a direct deal all the way. This was not broadly marketed," and added, "we absolutely would look for more opportunities like this."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, with repeated drilling into Triboro timing/cost and zoning mechanics (e.g., Borden asked about risk the council could "push that 180-day review even further out"), and multiple questions testing equity issuance conditions and disposition logic.

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and assertive, using confidence language on core initiatives, including Moragne on Triboro zoning: "We have all the confidence in the world that, that's where this will land" and "we expect to stay on the time line that we've announced without any hiccups."

  • Versus last quarter, management sounded less focused on relative valuation frustration and more focused on execution milestones; in Q1 Moragne emphasized index inclusion, saying, "we added an important milestone... our inclusion in the S&P 600 Index," while Q4 included, "we remain frustrated with our relative valuation."

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Build-to-suit pipeline commentary expanded from Q4’s "9 in-process developments" (Ryan Albano) to Q1’s "11 in-process developments" (President Albano) and Moragne’s updated visibility of "approximately $382 million of high-quality developments" into 2027.

  • Triboro detail increased materially: Q4 discussed a two-phase approach and early power delivery concepts, while Q1 added a utility schedule, with Albano stating, "PPL currently anticipates commencing construction in the summer of 2027 with completion targeted for the summer of 2030" and first energization of "300 megawatts" expected "between Q4 of 2027 and Q1 of 2028."

  • Equity issuance shifted from Q4’s smaller ATM totals ("approximately $43 million" forward basis) to Q1’s larger figure, as Moragne said, "we raised $71 million of equity under our ATM during the quarter" and total forward proceeds were "approximately $82.5 million."

Risks and concerns

  • Zoning process risk at Triboro was a central concern; Albano said the borough began a process "giving it up to 180 days" and that Broadstone filed a permit asserting the campus is "permitted by right," adding, "we remain confident in our path forward and do not expect any impact to our anticipated 2026 time line."

  • Tenant/watchlist monitoring remained in view; Moragne said on Red Lobster, "we continue to monitor them" and are looking for ways "to reduce that exposure over time," while Albano characterized watchlist activity as "relatively uneventful."

Final takeaway

Broadstone’s management described Q1 as continued momentum with $0.38 per-share AFFO, no loss rent in the quarter, and unchanged 2026 guidance of $1.53 to $1.57 per share while keeping a conservative 75-basis-point lost-rent assumption. The call’s biggest investor focus remained Project Triboro, where management provided expanded detail on zoning actions, near-term spend (under $15 million in 2026), and a longer power infrastructure timeline, while reiterating an end-of-2026 decision point on the project’s optimal path. Management also emphasized build-to-suit as the primary growth engine, citing a $382 million in-process pipeline, new investment-grade tenant projects, and measured, opportunity-set-driven equity issuance following S&P 600 inclusion.
